references.text: Chen, S-H & M-J Wu. 2007. A taxonomical study of the genus Boerhavia (Nyctaginaceae) in Taiwan. Taiwania 52: 332-342

Choo, LM, LMJ Chen & IM. Turner. 2022. Flora of Singapore precursors, 31: The genus Boerhavia (Nyctaginaceae) in Singapore and clarification of Boerhavia diffusa in Singapore and Peninsular Malaysia. Gardens' Bulletin Singapore 74: 19-36.
Rossetto, EFS & MA Caraballo-Ortiz. 2020. Splitting the Pisonia birdcatcher trees: re-establishment of Ceodes and Rockia (Nyctaginaceae, Pisonieae). PhytoKeys 152: 121-136
Shaw, HKA. 1952. On the distribution of Pisonia grandis R.Br. (Nyctaginaceae), with special reference to Malaysia. Kew Bulletin 7: 87-97.
Stemmerik, JF. 1964. Nyctaginaceae. Flora Malesiana series 1, 6: 450-468

genera.last_edited: Last edited by Pieter B. Pelser, 2 June 2026

species.citation: L. , Sp. Pl., 1st Ed. (1753) 3 ; Merr., Fl. Manila (1912) 197; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 133; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 454; Choo et al., Gard. Bull. Singapore 71 (2022) 24. = Boerhavia coccinea Mill. , Gard. Dict., 8th Ed. (1768) n.4.
species.notes: Weed in open places in and around towns, road sides, along streams and in open forest. 1m (photos).

species.citation: L. , Sp. Pl., 1st Ed. (1753) 3 ; Choo et al., Gard. Bull. Singapore 71 (2022) 28.
species.distribution: America. LUZON: Aurora (photos), Batangas (photos), Laguna, Rizal (photos).

species.citation: L. , Sp. Pl., 1st Ed. (1753) 3 ; Choo et al., Gard. Bull. Singapore 71 (2022) 30.
species.distribution: Africa, Andaman Isls, Australia, Bangladesh, Borneo, China, India, Indian Ocean, Japan, Java, Lesser Sunda Isls, Malay Peninsula, Middle East, Moluccas, Myanmar, New Guinea, Nicobar Isls, Pacific Ocean, Pakistan, ?Philippines, Ryukyu Isls, Solomon Isls, Sri Lanka, Sulawesi, Sumatra, Taiwan. ?LUZON: ?Batangas.
species.notes: Note by P.B. Pelser (4-Nov-23): a photo by Wally Suarez possibly represents this species in the Philippines.

species.citation: Holttum & Standl. , Publ. Field Mus. Nat. Hist., Bot. Ser. 23 (1944) 44 ; Pancho & Bardenas, Baileya 7 (1959) 99; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 456.
species.notes: Discovered in 1910 from Colombia, and taken into cultivation by many European firms. Introduced in Singapore in 1923. Now only known in cultivation.

species.citation: Choisy , Prodr. (A. P. de Candolle) 13(2) (1849) 437 ; Pancho & Bardenas, Baileya 7 (1959) 99; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 457.
species.notes: Flowered in Europe in 1860, mentioned from Bogor in 1866, from India in 1869, from Singapore in 1879. Very commonly planted. Flowers under everwet conditions.

species.citation: Bonpl. , Pl. Aequinoct. 1 (1808) 147 ; Pancho & Bardenas, Baileya 7 (1959) 97; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 456.
species.notes: Introduced in Singapore in 1938. Three garden varieties. Flowers a little after dry weather. Grows probably best on light soils.

species.citation: Willd. , Sp. Pl., 4th Ed. 2 (1799) 348, as 'Buginvillaea' ; Merr., Fl. Manila (1912) 196; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 133; Pancho & Bardenas, Baileya 7 (1959) 100; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 457.
species.notes: Introduced in Europe in 1829, recorded from Bogor in 1866, from Singapore in 1879. Very commonly planted. Flowers only in or in response to dry weather.

genera.last_edited: Last edited by Pieter B. Pelser, 10 May 2026

species.citation: (Teijsm. & Binn.) Merr. & L.M.Perry , J. Arnold Arbor. 20 (1939) 328 ; Rossetto & Caraballo-Ortiz, PhytoKeys 152 (2020) 131; ≡ Pisonia longirostris Teijsm. & Binn. , Natuurk. Tijdschr. Ned.-Indië 25 (1863) 401 ; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 134; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 463.
species.distribution: Lesser Sunda Isls, Moluccas, New Guinea, Philippines, Solomon Isls. JOLO, SULU ARCHIPELAGO, TAWI-TAWI.

species.citation: J.R.Forst. & G.Forst. , Char. Gen. Pl., 2nd Ed. (1776) 142 ; Rossetto & Caraballo-Ortiz, PhytoKeys 152 (2020) 132; ≡ Pisonia umbellifera (J.R.Forst. & G.Forst.) Seem. , Bonplandia 10 (1862) 154 ; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 134; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 460.
species.distribution: Africa, Andaman Isls, Australia, Bismarck Arch, China, Indian Ocean, Madagascar, New Zealand, Pacific Ocean, Philippines, Ryukyu Isls, Solomon Isls, Taiwan, Vietnam. LUZON: Apayao (photos), PALAUI (photos), PALAWAN, PANAY (photos).
species.notes: Throughout Malesia, particularly in the E part. 200m (photos).

genera.last_edited: Last edited by Pieter B. Pelser, 3 January 2024

species.citation: L. , Sp. Pl., 1st Ed. (1753) 177 ; Merr., Fl. Manila (1912) 197; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 132; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 451.
species.notes: Cultivated pantropically as an ornamental or medicinal plant. Occasionally escaping near towns. Cultivated up to c. 1400m. Flowers are ephemeral, opening at c. 4-4:30 pm (and closing at c. 9:00 am), hence the common names ‘alas-cuatro’ or ‘four o’clock’.

species.citation: L. , Sp. Pl., 1st Ed. (1753) 1026 ; Merr., Fl. Manila (1912) 196; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 133; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 467.
species.distribution: Africa, America, Andaman Isls, Australia, Borneo, China, India, Indian Ocean, Java, Laos, Lesser Sunda Isls, Madagascar, Malay Peninsula, New Caledonia, New Guinea, Philippines, Sri Lanka, Sulawesi, Sumatra, Taiwan, Vietnam. LUZON: Bataan, Batangas, Bulacan, Cagayan, Camarines Sur, Ilocos Norte, La Union, Laguna, Nueva Vizcaya, Pampanga, Pangasinan, Rizal, Tarlac, MINDANAO: Agusan del Norte, Zamboanga, MINDORO, PALAWAN.

species.citation: R.Br. , Prodr. Fl. Nov. Holland. (1810) 422 ; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 134; Shaw, Kew Bull. 7 (1952) 89; Stemmerik, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 6 (1964) 464. = Pisonia alba Span. , Linnaea 15 (1841) 342 ; Merr., Fl. Manila (1912) 196; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 133.
species.distribution: Africa, Andaman Isls, Australia, Borneo, India, Indian Ocean, Java, Lesser Sunda Isls, Madagascar, Malay Peninsula, Moluccas, New Caledonia, New Guinea, Nicobar Isls, Pacific Ocean, Philippines, Solomon Isls, Sri Lanka, Sulawesi, Taiwan. BANCORAN.
species.notes: Dry to semi-dry places, along coasts, sandy or rocky. Often dominant on isolated oceanic islets and atolls. Pisonia ‘alba’ is an almost echlorophyllose cultigen of Pisonia grandis. 0-1200m.
